description: Index of ethical reference frameworks used as anchors when reasoning about moral categories. Each entry identifies a school, its core principle, key authors for citation purposes, and the Hendrycks ETHICS categories where it is most relevant. These are NOT complete summaries of the theories — they are named reference points only.

schools.core_principle: Moral action centers on relationships, context, and responsiveness to the needs of particular others, not on abstract universal rules.
Ethical conduct flows from stable character traits (virtues) such as honesty, courage, justice, and practical wisdom, rather than from following rules or maximizing outcomes.
Actions are intrinsically right or wrong based on their conformity to duties and rules, independent of their consequences. Persons must be treated as ends in themselves, never merely as means.

Actions are evaluated by their tendency to produce the greatest overall wellbeing for all affected parties, while recognizing side-constraints that prevent treating persons merely as vessels for aggregate utility.

A just arrangement is one that rational persons would choose behind a veil of ignorance — not knowing their own place in the outcome. This favors fairness, equal basic liberties, and arrangements that benefit the least advantaged.
AI systems must be transparent, accountable, fair, inclusive, reliable, and privacy-preserving. They must support human oversight and avoid reinforcing discrimination or enabling misuse.
